tate of Cali 
air Political Practices CommissioIL 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
September 24, 1976 
John Gordnier 
Deputy Attorney General 
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 550 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear John: 
This letter is in response to your letter of August 31, 
1976, requesting our views on Attorney General opinion No. CR 76/42 IL. 
The numbering parallels the numbering in your letter. 
1. 
We assume that the contributions t.o which you 
refer were received without violating the prohibitions 
set forth in Gov. Code Section 84300, et seq., and that 
any redistribution of the funds also would be in compli-
ance with the strictures of these sections. 
If so, there 
are no restrictions in the Political Reform Act on the 
use of campaign contributions received by the treasurer 
of a campaign committee which has as its name "Committee 
to Elect John Doe Supervisor of San Diego County." 
In-
correct information provided in the Statement of Organi-
zation filed pursuant to Gov. Code Section 84101 arguably 
may be helpful in establishing fraud under certain cir-
cumstances, but we do not think this section or Section 
84102 impose a limitation on the use of ca~paign contri-
butions. 
However, we do think that it would be appropri-
ate for the Attorney General to develop guidelines con-
cerning the use of campaign funds. 
2. 
As stated above, there are no restrictions on 
the use of campaign contributions. 
However, the commit-
tee in question, by reason of its name, would appear to 
be a controlled committee or a primary purpose committee 
and, therefore, all of its activities are, by definition, 
political and hence all of its payments are reportable. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(b). 
3. 
Nothing in the Political Reform Act prevents 
the use of campaign contributions for strictly personal 
purposes. 
If, however, funds are used for personal, 
rather than political purposes, the committee, if it is 
a controlled committee or a primary purpose committee, 
still must report the 
i ture. 
In any event, the 
o 
icer must 
s 
s a gi 
on his 


John Gordnler 
September 24, 
Page Two 
Statement of Economic Interests. 
See Opinion Requested 
by Janet Gray Hayes, 1 FPPC Ops. 210 (No. 75-145, Dec. 
4, 1975). 
4. 
The answer to this question is the same as the 
answer to question 3. 
5. 
No, the answer to questions 1 through 4 would 
not be different if the name of the committee were 
changed. 
However, arguably the name of the committee 
may be relevant in determining whether or not fraud is 
present. 
(see answer to question 1, supra). 
6. 
All payments made by a controlled committee, 
official committee of a political party or an organi-
zation formed or existing primarily for political pur-
poses must be reported since, by definition, all pay-
ments made by such organizations are for political pur-
poses. 
See 2 Cal. Adm; Code Section 18225(b). 
With 
respect to all other organizations, only those payments 
used to make contributions or used in connection with 
a communication which expressly advocates a result in 
an election for or against a clearly identified can-
didate or measure need be reported. 
See 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Sections 18225(c) and (d). 
If the opinion issued 
by your office relative to the issues posed in this 
question, and in questions 7 and 8, reaches conclusions 
contrary to those outlined herein, we ask that your 
opinion, at least with respect to these questions, be 
issued as an indexed letter. 
7. 
Not applicable. 
8. 
Nothing in the Political Reform Act prevents 
a local governmental agency from imposing additional 
requirements on any person so long as the requirements 
do not prevent that person from complying with the 
provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
See Gov. Code 
Section 81013; Opinion requested by Edwin Miller, 
2 FPPC Ops. 91 (No. 75-125, July 6, 1976). 
When your opinion is adopted, we would appreciate re-
ceiving a copy of it. 
If you have any further questions, please 
feel free to contact either me or Ted Prim. 
Sincerely, 
~~~I~~ 
Michael Bennett 
Exe 
ve 
ctor 
MB:'I'P:jo 


EVEL.L.E J. YOUNGER 
ATTQRf'tEY GENERA.L. 
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
OFFICE OF THE ATTORNEY GENERAL 
1ltrpartmrnt of 3Juntirr 
555 CAPITOL. MAL.L.. SUITE 550 
SACRAMENTO 
95814 
(916) 445·9555 
August 31, 1976 
Mickey Bennett 
Executive Director 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 "K" Street Building 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Re: 
CR 76/42 I.L. 
Dear Mr. Bennett: 
Our office has received a request for an opinion 
on the following eight questions from the District Attorney 
of San Diego County: 
1. 
Are there any restrictions on the use of 
campaign contributions received by the 
treasurer of a committee which has as its 
name, "Committee to Elect John Doe Super-
visor of San Diego County"? 
2. 
Could such contributions be used for political 
purposes other than those set forth in the 
definition of "political purposes" contained 
in Section 18225, Division VI, Title 2, of 
the California Administrative Code? 
3. 
Could such contributions be converted to 
the personal use of the candidate (John 
Doe) for clearly non-political purposes? 


Mickey Bennett 
August 31, 1976 
Page 2 
4. 
Could such contributions be converted to 
the personal use of the campaign treasurer 
or anyone designated by him for clearly 
non-political purposes? 
5. 
Would the answers to questions 1 through 4 
be different if the name of the committee 
were "John Doe Political Fund" or some 
other name which does not expressly refer 
to a specific election? 
6. 
Assuming that an expenditure is made for a 
purpose other than a "political purpose" 
as defined in Section 18225, Division VI, 
Title 2, of the California Administrative 
Code, must the expenditure be reported in 
a campaign disclosure statement filed 
pursuant to Chapter 4 of the Political 
Reform Act? 
7. 
If the answer to question 6 is "no", is 
there any requirement that such expenditure 
be reported under any provision of state 
law? 
8. 
If there are no restrictions on use of 
campaign funds contained in state law, 
could a local legislative body restrict 
such use by ordinance? 
Could you please furnish any thoughts you might 
have with regard to these eight questions to me on or before 
September 10, 1976. 
Thank you for your consideration. 
JAG:dt 
Very truly yours, 
EVELLE J. YOUNGER 
Attorney General 
~c:2. ~~/~ 
~H~-A. GORDNIER 
J 
Deputy Attorney General

7.

8.

a local governmental agency from imposing additional 
requirements on any person so long as the requirements 
do not prevent that person from complying with the 
provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
See Gov. Code 
Section 81013; Opinion requested by Edwin Miller,

When your opinion is adopted, we would appreciate re-
ceiving a copy of it.
sections.conclusion: 1. No restrictions on campaign contribution use if received in compliance with Gov. Code Section 84300 et seq.; however, Attorney General should develop guidelines. 2. Committee appears to be controlled/primary purpose committee with all activities reportable. 3. Nothing prevents personal use of contributions, but if used for personal purposes by controlled committee, expenditure must be reported and officer must disclose on Statement of Economic Interests. 4. Same answer as question 3. 5. No difference with changed committee name. 6. All payments by controlled committees must be reported; other organizations report only contributions or communications expressly advocating election results. 7. Not applicable. 8. Local governmental agencies may impose additional requirements if they do not prevent compliance with Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: The Attorney General requested FPPC guidance on eight questions regarding restrictions on the use of campaign contributions, whether funds can be used for non-political purposes, reporting requirements for non-political expenditures, and whether local ordinances can impose additional restrictions on campaign fund use.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that while there are no express restrictions on campaign contribution use, contributions received in compliance with law have no use restrictions; however, controlled committees must report all expenditures regardless of purpose. Local ordinances may impose additional restrictions provided they do not conflict with state law compliance.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (August 31, 1976 letter from Deputy Attorney General Gordnier) and FPPC response (September 24, 1976 letter from Michael Bennett). Extracted structure based on FPPC response portion. Minor OCR issues in formatting but content is legible and complete.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter responds to the Attorney General's request for guidance on eight questions regarding campaign fund use restrictions, personal conversion of funds, reporting requirements, and local government authority. The FPPC advises that while no statutory restrictions exist on contribution use, controlled committees must report all expenditures and officers must disclose personal benefits on Statements of Economic Interests.
